sections.heading: Investment dealers – advances to
sections.title: Prohibition on advances to clients without contracts
sections.summary: This section prohibits investment dealers from making advances to clients for securities purchases without a pre-executed contract that complies with FSC Rules and without the advance amount being limited to a specified percentage of the market value of securities.

requirements.text: Client must execute contract before advance for securities purchase
Contract must comply with FSC Rules
Advance amount must not exceed percentage of market value of securities specified in FSC Rules

sections.source_quote: 51. Investment dealers – advances to clients (1) No investment dealer shall make an advance to his client by way of loan, to be applied to buy securities unless— (a) before the advance is made, the client has executed a contract that sets out the terms on which the advance is made and such contract complies with the FSC Rules; and (b) the amount advanced does not exceed the percentage of the market value of the securities specified in FSC Rules made for the purposes of this paragraph.
sections.relevance_reason: Financial data handling with KYC/AML constraints

sections.heading: Interpretation
sections.title: Key terms and definitions for securities regulation
sections.summary: This section provides comprehensive definitions for terms used throughout the Securities Act, including critical concepts like 'securities', 'investment dealer', 'securities exchange', and 'sophisticated investor'. These definitions establish the foundational language for regulatory compliance and market operations, ensuring clarity in the application of securities laws across different contexts.
sections.dtia_summary: These definitions are crucial for DTIAs as they specify the scope of data types and entities involved in financial transactions, directly impacting cross-border data flows and regulatory oversight.

requirements.text: Define 'securities' as shares, debentures, rights, warrants, options, and other transferable instruments
Define 'investment dealer' as a person holding an investment dealer licence
Define 'sophisticated investor' as entities including government bodies, banks, and investment dealers

sections.source_quote: 2. Interpretation In this Act—
sections.relevance_reason: Tangential definitions for financial terms

sections.heading: Interpretation (cont.)
sections.relevance_reason: pure amendment mechanics

sections.heading: Companies Act interpretation to apply
sections.title: Companies Act definitions apply
sections.summary: This section states that unless otherwise specified in the Securities Act, terms and expressions have the same meaning as in the Companies Act. This ensures consistency in legal terminology across different regulatory frameworks, particularly important for entities operating under both acts.
sections.dtia_summary: This section clarifies the scope of definitions by aligning the Securities Act with the Companies Act, which may affect how financial data is classified and handled across jurisdictions.

requirements.text: Terms in Securities Act have same meaning as in Companies Act unless otherwise provided

sections.source_quote: 4. Companies Act interpretation to apply Unless otherwise provided in this Act, words and expressions in this Act have the same meaning as in the Companies Act.
sections.relevance_reason: Terms consistency across acts

sections.heading: Objects of Commission
sections.title: Commission's regulatory objectives
sections.summary: This section outlines the key objectives of the Financial Services Commission in administering the Securities Act, including promoting investor confidence, protecting investors from fraud, fostering transparent markets, reducing systemic risk, and regulating disclosure practices. These objectives guide the Commission's regulatory activities and oversight mechanisms.
sections.dtia_summary: These objectives establish the regulatory framework for financial data handling, emphasizing transparency and risk management that directly influence data transfer and storage practices across borders.

requirements.text: Commission must promote confident and informed participation of investors in securities markets
Commission must improve protection of investors from unfair practices
Commission must foster fair, efficient, transparent and informed markets
Commission must reduce systemic risk in the financial sector
Commission must regulate disclosure of information by persons issuing securities
Commission must monitor and regulate securities exchanges and clearing services
Commission must suppress and prevent financial crimes
Commission must cooperate with domestic and international organizations
Commission must carry out research and disseminate data on the securities industry

sections.source_quote: 6. Objects of Commission (1) This Act shall be administered by the Commission.
sections.relevance_reason: Regulatory objectives for financial markets

sections.heading: Code of ethics
sections.title: Code of ethics for Commission staff
sections.summary: This section mandates that the Minister may create regulations for the Commission's staff code of ethics, covering areas such as acceptance of gifts, employment within the securities industry, acting as an officer of reporting issuers, and involvement in securities transactions. Violations may result in fines up to 500,000 rupees.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes ethical standards for Commission staff that impact data handling practices, particularly in cross-border financial transactions involving sensitive information.

requirements.text: Commission staff must follow code of ethics regarding acceptance of gifts
Commission staff must avoid employment within the securities industry
Commission staff must not act as officers of reporting issuers
Commission staff must not engage in securities transactions
Violations of code of ethics may result in fines up to 500,000 rupees

sections.source_quote: 8. Code of ethics (1) The Minister may, by regulations, make code of ethics for the members of the Board of the Commission, the Chief Executive and the staff of the Commission relating to—
sections.relevance_reason: Tangential governance for staff ethics

sections.heading: Securities exchange rules
sections.title: Securities exchange rulemaking authority
sections.summary: Securities exchanges can establish rules for operations, including access conditions, listing requirements, trading terms, enforcement procedures, conflict of interest management, buy-back rules, derivatives, and conduct standards. These rules must be approved by the Commission and function as binding contracts with issuers and market participants.
sections.dtia_summary: Rules for securities exchanges must be approved by the Commission and form binding contracts with market participants, affecting data transfer and processing within exchange systems.

requirements.text: Securities exchanges may make rules consistent with this Act, regulations, or FSC Rules
Rules may specify access terms including financial integrity and business ethics conditions
Rules may define listing conditions for securities
Rules may establish trading conditions for securities
Rules may include enforcement procedures and sanctions
Rules may address conflict of interest management
Rules may cover buy-back conditions for listed securities
Rules may regulate derivatives contracts and settlement procedures
Rules must implement Commission directives
Rules must specify conduct standards for exchange officers and committee members
Rules must be approved by the Commission
Rules form binding contracts with issuers and market participants

sections.source_quote: 13. Securities exchange rules (1) A securities exchange may make rules, not inconsistent with this Act, any regulations made under this Act or any FSC Rules, for or with respect to the operation of the securities exchange.
sections.relevance_reason: Rules define financial data handling but not directly data protection

sections.heading: Annual reports
sections.title: Annual financial reporting requirements
sections.summary: Licensed entities must file audited annual reports within 90 days of their balance sheet date, including corporate governance reports, IFRS-compliant financial statements, and consolidated financial statements where applicable. Financial statements must be audited by Commission-approved firms following International Standards on Auditing.
sections.dtia_summary: Annual reporting requirements affect data storage and financial data handling, with specific audit and governance standards impacting data transfer and processing.

requirements.text: Licensed entities must file annual reports within 90 days of balance sheet date
Annual reports must include corporate governance policy reports
Annual reports must include IFRS-compliant financial statements
Annual reports must include consolidated financial statements for holding companies or subsidiaries
Financial statements must be audited by Commission-approved firms
Clearing facilities must include risk management reports

sections.source_quote: 20. Annual reports (1) A person licensed under this Sub-Part shall file with the Commission, within 90 days of its balance sheet date, an annual report which shall include— (a) a report on the corporate governance policy of the licensee and any other information required by the Commission; (b) audited financial statements prepared in accordance with IFRS and such other standards issued under the Financial Reporting Act and such other requirements as may be specified in the FSC Rules; and (c) consolidated financial statements, where the person is a holding company or a subsidiary.
sections.relevance_reason: Financial reporting mandates with audit requirements

sections.heading: Regulatory functions
sections.title: Market supervision and regulatory oversight
sections.summary: The Securities and Markets Authority (SEM) must supervise market operations, ensure internal controls, enforce capital adequacy rules, investigate misconduct and market abuses, and report findings to the Commission. SEM must also maintain adequate budgets and submit regulatory activity reports in annual filings.
sections.dtia_summary: SEM's regulatory oversight directly impacts data processing and storage requirements for market participants, with specific investigation and reporting obligations affecting data transfer.

requirements.text: SEM must supervise market operations and participant conduct
SEM must ensure adequate internal controls
SEM must ensure market participants follow capital adequacy rules
SEM must investigate misconduct affecting investors and report to Commission
SEM must investigate market abuses including insider dealing and fraud
SEM must take disciplinary measures after public hearing
SEM must inform Commission about investigations and involved persons
SEM must report investigation status to Commission at intervals requested
SEM must transmit all investigation information to Commission upon completion
SEM must inform Commission of investigation outcomes and disciplinary actions
SEM must ensure adequate budget for regulatory functions
SEM must submit regulatory activity information in annual reports

sections.source_quote: 24. Regulatory functions (1) In addition to its other functions, the SEM shall have regulatory functions and shall— (a) ensure that it adequately supervises the market operations and the conduct of market participants; (b) ensure the adequacy and efficiency of internal controls; (c) ensure that market participants follow capital adequacy rules; (d) investigate misconduct or apparent misconduct by market participants and their representatives that could seriously affect investors or other participating organisations and promptly report to the Commission on these instances of misconduct; (e) investigate possible market abuses, including insider dealing and fraudulent behaviour; (f) take disciplinary measures or agree to a settlement after a public hearing.
sections.relevance_reason: Regulator oversight with data handling implications

sections.heading: Transfer of ownership of securities
sections.title: Securities ownership transfer rules
sections.summary: The Central Depository System (CDS) is the exclusive method for transferring securities ownership, with its records prevailing over any conflicting external records. This establishes a single authoritative source for securities ownership data.
sections.dtia_summary: CDS records are the sole authority for securities ownership, affecting data transfer and storage within the depository system.

requirements.text: Securities ownership transfers must follow CDS rules and procedures
CDS records prevail over any conflicting external records

sections.source_quote: 28. Transfer of ownership of securities (1) Notwithstanding any other enactment, the exclusive method of transferring the ownership of securities deposited in the CDS shall be a transfer made in accordance with the rules and procedures of the CDS.
sections.relevance_reason: CDS rules define financial data handling for securities transfers

sections.heading: Licence for investment dealers and their representatives
sections.title: Investment dealer licensing requirements
sections.summary: No person may act as an investment dealer or intermediary without a Commission-issued license, including trading securities for their own account or underwriting securities. Only corporate entities may apply for investment dealer licenses, with additional rules for authorized activities.
sections.dtia_summary: Investment dealers must be licensed by the Commission before handling securities, affecting data transfer and processing with third parties.

requirements.text: No person may act as an investment dealer without Commission license
No person may trade securities for their own account without Commission license
No person may underwrite securities without Commission license
Only corporate entities may apply for investment dealer licenses

sections.source_quote: 29. Licence for investment dealers and their representatives (1) Subject to section 79A of the Financial Services Act, no person shall, by way of business— (a) act or hold himself out as an intermediary in the execution of securities transactions on behalf of other persons; (b) trade or hold himself out to trade in securities as principal for his own account with the intention of selling them to the public; or (c) underwrite or distribute or hold himself out to underwrite or distribute securities on behalf of an issuer or a holder of securities, without an investment dealer licence issued by the Commission or without being licensed as a representative of an investment dealer by the Commission.
sections.relevance_reason: Licensing for financial data handling

sections.heading: Licence for investment advisers and their representatives
sections.title: Investment adviser licensing requirements
sections.summary: No person may provide investment advice or manage portfolios without a Commission-issued license, including advice on securities transactions and corporate finance matters. Only corporate entities may apply for investment adviser licenses.
sections.dtia_summary: Investment advisers must be licensed by the Commission before providing financial advice, affecting data transfer and processing with clients.

requirements.text: No person may advise on securities transactions without Commission license
No person may manage securities portfolios without Commission license
No person may provide corporate finance advice without Commission license
Only corporate entities may apply for investment adviser licenses

sections.source_quote: 30. Licence for investment advisers and their representatives Subject to section 79A of the Financial Services Act, no person shall, by way of business— (a) advise, guide or recommend other persons, or hold himself out to advise, guide or recommend other persons, whether personally or through printed materials or by other means, to enter into securities transactions; (b) manage or hold himself out to manage, under a mandate, whether discretionary or not, a portfolio of securities; or (c) give advice on corporate finance advisory matters concerning securities transactions, without an investment adviser licence issued by the Commission or without being licensed as a representative of an investment adviser by the Commission.
sections.relevance_reason: Licensing requirements for financial data handling

sections.heading: Responsibility for conduct of representatives
sections.title: Responsibility for conduct of representatives
sections.summary: This section establishes that investment dealers or advisers are responsible for the conduct of their representatives when such conduct relates to securities, involves third parties who could reasonably rely on it, or where the client actually relied on it in good faith. The responsibility applies regardless of whether the representative was acting within their authority.

requirements.text: Conduct of a representative relates to securities
Conduct on which a client could reasonably be expected to rely
Conduct on which the client in fact relied in good faith
Investment dealer or adviser is responsible for representative conduct regardless of authority
Responsibility does not apply if conduct was not related to securities
Representative must clearly disclose non-securities conduct to client before reliance

sections.source_quote: 32. Responsibility for conduct of representatives (1) This section applies to conduct of a representative— (a) that relates to securities; (b) on which a third person (the "client") could reasonably be expected to rely; and (c) on which the client in fact relied in good faith. (2) The investment dealer or the investment adviser shall be responsible for the conduct of its representatives whether or not the representative was acting within his authority.
sections.relevance_reason: Responsibility for conduct of representatives in securities context

sections.heading: Capital and liquidity requirements
sections.title: Capital and liquidity requirements for investment dealers
sections.summary: This section mandates that the Financial Services Commission (FSC) may establish capital and liquidity requirements for investment dealers and advisers. The Commission must consider the nature of the licensed activity and the risks posed to clients or the securities market when formulating these rules.

requirements.text: FSC Rules may provide capital and liquidity requirements for investment dealers and advisers
Commission must consider nature of licensed activity when formulating rules
Commission must consider risks posed to clients or securities market when formulating rules

sections.source_quote: 50. Capital and liquidity requirements (1) The FSC Rules may provide for capital and liquidity requirements and other prudential rules for investment dealers and investment advisers. (2) In formulating rules for the purposes of subsection (1), the matters to which the Commission shall have regard include— (a) the nature of the activity authorised by the relevant licence; and (b) the nature and extent of the risks that those licensees are exposed to, and that they pose to their clients or to the integrity of the securities market.
sections.relevance_reason: FSC rules on capital/liquidity for financial entities

sections.heading: Investment dealers – advances to clients
sections.title: Prohibition on advances to clients without contracts
sections.summary: This section prohibits investment dealers from making advances to clients for securities purchases without a pre-executed contract that complies with FSC Rules and without the advance amount being limited to a specified percentage of the market value of securities.

requirements.text: Client must execute contract before advance for securities purchase
Contract must comply with FSC Rules
Advance amount must not exceed percentage of market value of securities specified in FSC Rules

sections.source_quote: 51. Investment dealers – advances to clients (1) No investment dealer shall make an advance to his client by way of loan, to be applied to buy securities unless— (a) before the advance is made, the client has executed a contract that sets out the terms on which the advance is made and such contract complies with the FSC Rules; and (b) the amount advanced does not exceed the percentage of the market value of the securities specified in FSC Rules made for the purposes of this paragraph.
sections.relevance_reason: Financial data handling with KYC/AML constraints

sections.heading: Custody services
sections.title: Custody services authorization conditions
sections.summary: This section authorizes investment dealers to offer custody services to clients if they meet specific conditions related to the security and safety of physical securities as defined by FSC Rules.

requirements.text: Investment dealer must satisfy conditions relating to security and safety of physical securities
Conditions must be specified in FSC Rules

sections.source_quote: 52. Custody services An investment dealer who satisfies such conditions relating to security and safety of physical securities as may be specified in FSC Rules may be authorised under its licence to offer custody services to his clients.
sections.relevance_reason: FSC Rules define security conditions for physical securities

sections.heading: Underwriting
sections.title: Underwriting services authorization conditions
sections.summary: This section authorizes investment dealers to offer underwriting services to clients if they meet specific conditions related to capital, infrastructure, and security as defined by FSC Rules.

requirements.text: Investment dealer must satisfy conditions relating to capital, infrastructure and security
Conditions must be specified in FSC Rules

sections.source_quote: 53. Underwriting An investment dealer who satisfies such conditions relating to capital, infrastructure and security as may be specified in FSC Rules may be authorised under its licence to offer underwriting services.
sections.relevance_reason: Security and KYC requirements

sections.heading: Credit balances
sections.title: Credit balances management rules
sections.summary: This section specifies that credit balances in client accounts not representing pledged securities must be payable on demand, not used by the investment dealer without client consent, and not part of the dealer's insolvency assets. Non-compliance incurs a fine of up to 500,000 rupees.

requirements.text: Credit balances must be payable on demand
Credit balances must not be used by investment dealer without client written authority
Credit balances must not form part of investment dealer's insolvency assets

sections.source_quote: 54. Credit balances (1) Any credit balances in the accounts of a client of an investment dealer, not representing securities that are pledged, mortgaged, subject to a lien or other security interest or given to support a guarantee or similar arrangement, shall— (a) be payable on demand; (b) not be used or applied by the investment dealer without the express written authority of the client; and (c) not form part of the assets of the investment dealer for the purposes of the law relating to insolvency.
sections.relevance_reason: Financial data handling with penalties

sections.heading: Annual reports
sections.title: Annual reports filing for investment dealers and advisers
sections.summary: Investment dealers and investment advisers licensed under this Act must file annual reports with the Commission within 90 days of their balance sheet date. The reports must include corporate governance policies, audited financial statements prepared under IFRS, consolidated financial statements if applicable, and any other requirements specified in FSC Rules. Audit firms must be approved by the Commission and meet experience, expertise, and resource standards to conduct audits.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires audited financial statements for investment dealers and advisers, with specific reporting timelines and governance standards that impact cross-border financial data transfers involving financial institutions.

requirements.text: Investment dealers and investment advisers must file annual reports with the Commission within 90 days of their balance sheet date
Annual reports must include corporate governance policies and any other information required by the Commission
Annual reports must include audited financial statements prepared in accordance with IFRS and relevant standards
Annual reports must include consolidated financial statements if the investment dealer or adviser is a holding company or subsidiary
Audit firms must be approved by the Commission and have adequate experience, expertise, and resources

sections.source_quote: 55. Annual reports (1) An investment dealer and a corporation licensed as an investment adviser shall file with the Commission, within 90 days of its balance sheet date, an annual report which shall include— (a) a report on the corporate governance policy of the licensee and any other information required by the Commission; (b) audited financial statements prepared in accordance with IFRS and such other standards as may be issued under the Financial Reporting Act; (c) such other requirements as may be specified in FSC Rules; and (d) consolidated financial statements where the investment dealer or investment adviser is a holding company or a subsidiary.
sections.relevance_reason: Audit firms must be approved by Commission

sections.heading: Securities transaction confirmations
sections.title: Securities transaction confirmations and statements
sections.summary: Investment dealers must send transaction confirmations and account statements to clients without delay in specified formats. They cannot trade as principal in listed securities without following exchange rules. For unlisted securities, dealers must disclose principal trading to clients before executing transactions. Violations incur fines up to 100,000 rupees.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires real-time transaction confirmations and disclosures for securities transactions, impacting data transfer timelines and transparency for cross-border financial activities.

requirements.text: Investment dealers must send transaction confirmations to clients without delay in specified formats
Investment dealers must send account statements to clients at intervals specified in FSC Rules
Investment dealers cannot trade as principal in listed securities without following exchange rules
For unlisted securities, investment dealers must disclose principal trading to clients before executing transactions

sections.source_quote: 56. Securities transaction confirmations (1) Where an investment dealer executes an order of a client to carry out a securities transaction, it shall send to its client without delay, a confirmation in such form as may be specified in the FSC Rules. (2) Every investment dealer shall send to its client a statement of account in such form and at such intervals as may be specified in the FSC Rules.
sections.relevance_reason: Tangential: financial data handling without direct privacy/cross-border focus

sections.heading: Prospectus not required
sections.title: Exemptions for prospectus requirements
sections.summary: Certain securities offerings do not require a prospectus, including private placements, offers to sophisticated investors, related corporations, specific employee share plans, and amalgamations. Issuers must notify the Commission within 10 days for private placements and comply with specific timelines for other exemptions. The Commission may reject exemptions if requirements are not met.
sections.dtia_summary: Provides exemptions from prospectus requirements for specific financial transactions, affecting data transfer obligations when securities are issued without formal disclosure.

requirements.text: No prospectus required for securities issued at company formation without solicitation
No prospectus required for securities transmission by succession
No prospectus required for securities transfers by operation of law or court order
No prospectus required for private placements of securities
No prospectus required for offers to sophisticated investors
No prospectus required for offers to related corporations
No prospectus required for specific employee share plans with compliance
No prospectus required for amalgamations of companies
Issuer must notify Commission within 10 days for private placements

sections.source_quote: 70. Prospectus not required (1) A prospectus shall not be needed for— (a) an issue of securities of a company at or in connection with the formation of the company, where no solicitation is made for the purchase of the securities; (b) the transmission of securities by succession; (c) the vesting or transfer of securities by operation of law or by order of a Court; (d) an offer or issue of securities that is a private placement; (e) an offer or issue of securities that is made only to sophisticated investors; (f) an offer or issue of securities only to related corporations of the issuer of the securities; (g) an offer by an issuer— (i) to allow the exercise of an exchange, conversion, or subscription rights previously issued for securities held by a reporting issuer; (ii) under a subscription plan, a share dividend plan or a dividend reinvestment plan; or (iii) under an employee share plan or a similar plan and is made only to officers or employees of the issuer, where the issuer has complied with its obligations under this Act, any regulations made under this Act or any FSC Rules as to disclosure in relation to the securities; (h) an offer of securities acquired under an offer referred to in subsection (1) (d) or (e) where— (i) a sale of securities is made in normal market operations on a securities exchange; (ii) the issuer has complied with its obligations under this Act, any regulations made under this Act or any FSC Rules and the rules of the securities exchange as to disclosure in relation to the securities; and (iii) the person making the offer has held the securities for at least the period specified in FSC Rules; (i) an offer or issue of securities for the purpose of effecting an amalgamation of companies.
sections.relevance_reason: No data protection or cross-border transfer relevance

sections.heading: General requirements
sections.title: Prospectus content requirements
sections.summary: Prospectuses must provide full, true, and plain disclosure of material facts about the issuer and securities, including financial position, profits, and rights attached to securities. They must include the prospectus date, signed director statements accepting responsibility, required signatures, and a disclaimer of Commission responsibility.
sections.dtia_summary: Mandates comprehensive disclosure in prospectuses, impacting data transfer transparency and compliance for cross-border financial offerings.

requirements.text: Prospectuses must provide full, true, and plain disclosure of material facts about the issuer and securities
Prospectuses must include the date of the prospectus as the provisional registration date
Prospectuses must contain a statement signed by all directors accepting responsibility for contents
Prospectuses must include required signatures as specified in FSC Rules
Prospectuses must contain a statement that the Commission takes no responsibility for contents

sections.source_quote: 71. General requirements (1) A prospectus shall provide full, true and plain disclosure of all material facts concerning the securities to be offered and the person offering the securities, without omitting anything that would be required to allow investors to make an informed assessment of— (a) the assets and liabilities, financial position, profits and losses and prospects of the issuer of the securities; and (b) the rights and liabilities attaching to the securities. (2) A prospectus shall contain— (a) the date of the prospectus, which shall be the date on which the prospectus is provisionally registered with the Commission; (b) a statement signed by all the directors of the issuer to the effect that they accept responsibility for the contents of the prospectus and that, to the best of their knowledge and belief, and after making reasonable inquiries, the prospectus complies with this Act, any regulations made under this Act or any FSC Rules; (c) such signatures as may be specified in FSC Rules; and (d) a statement to the effect that the Commission takes no responsibility for its contents.
sections.relevance_reason: Financial disclosure requirements for prospectuses

sections.heading: Consent to certain statements
sections.title: Consent for statements in prospectuses
sections.summary: Statements in prospectuses must have consent from the person making the statement, with the prospectus stating consent, filed consent with the Commission, and no withdrawal of consent before the prospectus is lodged for provisional registration.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires explicit consent for statements in prospectuses, ensuring data accuracy and compliance when transferring financial information across borders.

requirements.text: Statements in prospectuses must have consent from the person making the statement
Prospectuses must state that the person has given consent
Consent must be filed with the Commission
The person must not have withdrawn consent before the prospectus is lodged for provisional registration

sections.source_quote: 72. Consent to certain statements (1) A prospectus shall not include any statement made by a person, or any statement said in the prospectus to be based on a statement made by a person, unless— (a) the person has consented to the statement being included in the prospectus in the form and context in which it appears or referred to in the prospectus; (b) the prospectus states that the person has given his consent; (c) the consent is filed with the Commission; and (d) the person has not withdrawn his consent before the date the prospectus is lodged for provisional registration with the Commission.
sections.relevance_reason: Pure financial regulation, no data protection or cross-border transfer relevance

sections.heading: Registration of prospectuses
sections.title: Prospectus registration requirements
sections.summary: Prospectuses must be registered with the Commission, including providing a certified copy of director statements, meeting disclosure requirements, and complying with specific timelines for registration and amendments. Pending registration, certain activities like subscription applications and interest indications are restricted.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires registration of prospectuses with specific disclosures and restrictions on activities during pending registration, impacting data transfer compliance for cross-border financial offerings.

requirements.text: Commission issues acknowledgement of filing on terms and conditions it deems appropriate
Prospectuses must be accompanied by a true certified copy of the director statement
No securities advertisement may be published without a prospectus with acknowledgement of filing
Advertisements must state that applications can only be made after prospectus registration
Prospectuses must be available for public inspection
Advertisements must provide the address for prospectus inspection
Commission may grant registration if prospectus meets disclosure requirements
Pending registration, copies of prospectuses may be transmitted free of charge to subscribers
No subscription application forms may accompany prospectuses during pending registration
No subscription offers may be entertained during pending registration
No interest indications may be accepted except without firm commitment
Only licensed investment dealers may accept interest indications

sections.source_quote: 76. Registration of prospectuses (1) The Commission shall, on filing of a prospectus, issue an acknowledgement of filing on such terms and conditions as it may deem appropriate. (2) The prospectus shall be accompanied by a true certified copy of the statement referred to in section 71 (2) (b). (3) No person shall publish, or cause to be published, a securities advertisement for which a prospectus is required unless— (a) a prospectus in respect of the offer has been issued with an acknowledgement of filing; (b) the advertisement states that applications for subscription may only be made after the prospectus has been issued with an acknowledgement of filing and on an application form attached to the prospectus; (c) the prospectus is available for public inspection; and (d) the advertisement gives the address of the place where the prospectus can be inspected or requested. (4) The Commission may, after a review based on disclosure requirements and not on the merits of the offer, grant a registration where— (a) the person making the offer has made any required amendment to the prospectus; (b) the Commission is satisfied that the prospectus meets all disclosure requirements of the Act, any regulations made under this Act or the FSC Rules; (c) consents mentioned in section 72 have been received; and (d) a prospectus in its final form as determined by the FSC Rules has been filed with the Commission.
sections.relevance_reason: Tangential: financial data governance

sections.heading: Civil liability on defective prospectuses
sections.title: Civil liability for misleading prospectuses
sections.summary: Any person harmed by misleading statements or material omissions in a prospectus can claim compensation from the issuer, directors, statement makers, underwriters, signatories, or those knowingly involved. Defences include reasonable inquiries and belief in no omission. Proceedings must be initiated within one year of the cause of action, with exceptions for court extensions. This section does not override other liabilities.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires identifying misleading statements or omissions in prospectuses to avoid cross-border liability claims; critical for assessing risks when transferring financial data internationally.

requirements.text: Any person who suffers loss or damage due to misleading statements or material omissions in a prospectus may recover from the issuer, directors, statement makers, underwriters, signatories, or those knowingly involved.
Defence requires proving reasonable inquiries and belief in no omission.
Proceedings must be initiated within one year of the cause of action, extendable by court.

sections.source_quote: 82. Any person who suffers loss or damage because— (a) a prospectus includes a misleading or deceptive statement; or (b) there is an omission from a prospectus and the omission is materially adverse from the point of view of an investor in those securities, may recover the amount of the loss or damage from— (i) the issuer or proposed issuer; (ii) each director; (iii) the maker of the statement; (iv) any investment dealer acting as underwriter; (v) any person who has signed the prospectus; or (vi) any person knowingly concerned in making the misleading or deceptive statement or omitting the material from the prospectus.
sections.relevance_reason: Mauritius Securities Act focuses on financial liability, not data protection

sections.heading: Right to withdraw and have money returned
sections.title: Right to withdraw securities without prospectus
sections.summary: Securities issued without a prospectus or proper disclosure documents are void, allowing the recipient to return securities and reclaim application money within one month. Directors are personally liable for repayment if the issuer fails to pay within seven days. Applies to both issues and sales of securities.
sections.dtia_summary: Ensures investors can reclaim funds if securities are issued without proper disclosure, relevant for data transfers involving financial instruments.

requirements.text: Securities issued without a prospectus or required disclosure documents are void.
Recipient may return securities and reclaim application money within one month.
Issuer must pay application money within seven days or directors are personally liable.

sections.source_quote: 83. Where securities are issued to a person without a prospectus or any other disclosure document required under this Part, or otherwise than on an application form attached to the prospectus, such issue shall be void and the person shall have the right to return the securities and to have its application money repaid.
sections.relevance_reason: Pure financial regulation, no data protection or cross-border transfer relevance

sections.heading: Persons subject to disclosure requirements
sections.title: Reporting issuers subject to disclosure
sections.summary: Reporting issuers include those who have issued securities via prospectus, made takeover offers, have securities listed on Mauritian exchanges, or have at least 100 shareholders. All such entities must comply with disclosure requirements, and the Commission maintains a public register indicating compliance status.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines entities subject to disclosure obligations, critical for determining which organizations require cross-border data transfers to comply with international disclosure standards.

requirements.text: Reporting issuer means an issuer who has made a prospectus offer, made a takeover offer, has securities listed on a Mauritian exchange, or has at least 100 shareholders.
Every reporting issuer must comply with disclosure requirements.
Commission maintains a public register of reporting issuers indicating compliance status.

sections.source_quote: 86. 'Reporting issuer' means an issuer who— (a) by way of a prospectus has made an offer of securities before or after the commencement of this Act; (b) has made a takeover offer by way of exchange of securities; (c) whose securities are listed on a securities exchange in Mauritius; or (d) has not less than 100 shareholders.
sections.relevance_reason: Regulator's public compliance register

sections.heading: Timely disclosure
sections.title: Timely disclosure of material changes
sections.summary: Reporting issuers must immediately disclose material changes affecting securities value, including distributions, ownership changes, name changes, mergers, asset acquisitions, capital structure changes, and other significant events. Disclosures must be in plain language, filed with the Commission, and may be issued after market close with trading halts if needed. Certain changes exempt from disclosure if they are incomplete, speculative, internal, or trade secrets.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires immediate disclosure of material changes to securities, critical for assessing data transfer risks when cross-border financial data is involved.

requirements.text: Reporting issuers must immediately issue press releases for material changes likely to affect securities value.
Press releases must be in plain language for investor understanding.
Press releases must be filed with the Commission immediately.
For listed securities, press releases must be filed with exchanges and may be issued after market close with trading halts.
Acquisitions or disposals exceeding 10% of net assets require disclosure.
Exemptions apply for incomplete proposals, speculative information, internal matters, and trade secrets.

sections.source_quote: 87. Where a material change occurs in the affairs of a reporting issuer that is likely to have a significant influence on the value or market price of its securities, the reporting issuer shall immediately issue a press release disclosing the change, unless such disclosure would amount to a criminal offence.
sections.relevance_reason: Financial disclosure requirements with exemptions

sections.heading: Periodical disclosure
sections.title: Periodical financial disclosures
sections.summary: Reporting issuers must file quarterly financial statements within 45 days of quarter-end and annual reports within 90 days of the balance sheet date, prepared in accordance with IFRS and audited. Consolidated statements apply to companies with subsidiaries, and annual reports must be approved by the board of directors.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires regular financial disclosures, critical for assessing data transfer risks when financial data is shared across borders.

requirements.text: Reporting issuers must file quarterly financial statements prepared in accordance with IFRS within 45 days of quarter-end.
Quarterly financial statements need not be audited.
Reporting issuers must file annual reports with audited financial statements within 90 days of balance sheet date.
Financial statements for companies with subsidiaries must be consolidated.
Annual reports must be approved by the board of directors.

sections.source_quote: 88. Every reporting issuer shall file with the Commission and make public comparative quarterly financial statements prepared in accordance with IFRS and such other standards as may be issued under the Financial Reporting Act, as soon as possible, but not later than 45 days after the end of each quarter.
sections.relevance_reason: Tangential: financial reporting governance

sections.heading: Duties of auditors
sections.title: Auditor reporting duties
sections.summary: Auditors of reporting issuers must report to the Commission in writing if they become aware of material adverse changes, potential contraventions of the Act, financial crimes, or serious irregularities. This duty is not breached if the auditor communicates information in good faith.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires auditors to report material issues to regulators, critical for identifying cross-border data transfer risks.

requirements.text: Auditors must report to the Commission in writing if they believe there has been a material adverse change affecting the issuer's ability to continue as a going concern.
Auditors must report if the issuer may be in contravention of the Act, regulations, FSC Rules, or Commission directions.
Auditors must report if financial crimes have occurred, are occurring, or are likely to occur.
Auditors must report if serious irregularities have occurred.
No breach of duty occurs if the auditor communicates information in good faith.

sections.source_quote: 89. Where in the course of his work, the auditor of a reporting issuer, other than a collective investment scheme, becomes aware of any matter which is such as to give the auditor reasonable cause to believe that— (a) there has been a material adverse change in the risks inherent in the business of a reporting issuer with the potential to jeopardise the ability of the reporting issuer to continue as a going concern; (b) the reporting issuer may be in contravention of this Act, any regulations made under this Act, any FSC Rules or any directions issued by the Commission; (c) a financial crime has been, is being or is likely to be committed; or (d) serious irregularities have occurred, the auditor shall report such matter in writing to the Commission.
sections.relevance_reason: Auditor reporting duties for financial crimes and irregularities

sections.heading: Notification of insiders' interests
sections.title: Insiders' interest notifications to reporting issuers
sections.summary: Insiders of reporting issuers must notify the issuer in writing within one month of acquiring securities interests, or immediately upon initial interest at the Act's commencement. The notification must follow specified FSC Rules forms. This applies to both initial interests and subsequent acquisitions, including those that make non-insiders into insiders.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires written notifications of insider securities interests to reporting issuers within specific timeframes, impacting data sharing protocols for insider information across borders.

requirements.text: Insiders must notify reporting issuers in writing of securities interests within one month of acquisition
Insiders must notify reporting issuers in writing of securities interests at commencement of Act
Subsection (2) applies to non-insiders who become insiders through acquisition

sections.source_quote: 90. Where, at the commencement of this Act, an insider of a reporting issuer or any of his associates, has an interest in the securities of the reporting issuer or of an associate of the reporting issuer, that insider shall give written notice of the interest to the reporting issuer in such form as may be specified in FSC Rules.
sections.relevance_reason: Financial data reporting obligations

sections.heading: Notification of changes in insiders' interests
sections.title: Insiders' interest changes notifications
sections.summary: Insiders must notify reporting issuers in writing within 14 days of any change in their securities interests or those of their associates. This includes cases where an insider ceases to be an insider due to the change in interest.
sections.dtia_summary: Mandates timely notifications of interest changes to reporting issuers, affecting data sharing timelines for insider information across borders.

requirements.text: Insiders must notify reporting issuers in writing of interest changes within 14 days
Subsection (1) applies to insiders who cease to be insiders due to interest changes

sections.source_quote: 91. An insider of a reporting issuer shall, within 14 days, notify the issuer of any change of his interest or that of any of his associates in the securities of the reporting issuer or of any associate of the reporting issuer in such form as may be specified in FSC Rules.
sections.relevance_reason: Financial data reporting with specific timelines

sections.heading: Notification to securities exchanges and Commission
sections.title: Insider notifications to exchanges and Commission
sections.summary: Reporting issuers must forward copies of insider notifications to the Commission and relevant securities exchange within 24 hours of receiving the notification. This notification may be published per FSC Rules or exchange rules.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires reporting issuers to share insider notifications with regulatory bodies and exchanges, impacting data flow and transparency for cross-border securities information.

requirements.text: Reporting issuers must send copies of insider notifications to Commission within 24 hours
Reporting issuers must send copies of insider notifications to relevant securities exchange within 24 hours
Insider notifications may be published per FSC Rules or exchange rules

sections.source_quote: 92. Where an issuer is given a notice under section 90 or 91, the issuer shall, before the end of the day following the day of notification, give a copy of the notice to— (a) the Commission; (b) the exchange where the securities of the reporting issuer are listed on a securities exchange.
sections.relevance_reason: Regulator powers and reporting duties

sections.heading: Penalty for contravention of regulations or FSC Rules
sections.title: Penalties for regulatory non-compliance
sections.summary: Contravention of regulations or FSC Rules constitutes an offence punishable by a fine up to one million rupees. Regulations may specify daily penalties for repeated non-compliance beyond specified timeframes.
sections.dtia_summary: Imposes financial penalties for non-compliance with insider notification rules, affecting compliance costs and risk management for cross-border data transfers.

requirements.text: Contravention of regulations or FSC Rules constitutes an offence punishable by a fine up to one million rupees
Regulations may specify daily penalties for repeated non-compliance beyond specified timeframes

sections.source_quote: 96. Any person who contravenes or fails to comply with any regulations or any FSC Rules made for the purposes of this Part shall commit an offence and shall, on conviction, be liable to a fine not exceeding one million rupees.
sections.relevance_reason: Penalties for non-compliance with securities regulations

sections.heading: CIS managers
sections.title: CIS manager licensing requirements
sections.summary: CIS managers must hold a valid CIS manager licence and comply with its conditions. The Commission requires applicants to be corporations, meet financial resource requirements, and be fit and proper persons.
sections.dtia_summary: Licensing requirements for CIS managers impact data handling protocols for collective investment schemes across borders.

requirements.text: CIS managers must hold and comply with CIS manager licence conditions
Applicants must be body corporates
Commission must be satisfied applicant meets financial resource requirements
Applicants and officers must be fit and proper persons

sections.source_quote: 98. No person shall act as a CIS manager for a collective investment scheme or hold himself out to act as a CIS manager for a collective investment scheme unless the person holds and is complying with the conditions of a CIS manager licence.
sections.relevance_reason: Licensing conditions for financial data handling

sections.heading: CIS administrators
sections.title: CIS administrator approvals
sections.summary: CIS administrators must obtain Commission approval before providing administration services to collective investment schemes. Administrators must be corporations and meet fit and proper criteria.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires Commission approval for CIS administrators, affecting data handling and service provision across borders for collective investment schemes.

requirements.text: CIS administrators must obtain Commission approval before providing services
Applicants must be body corporates
Commission must be satisfied applicants are fit and proper persons

sections.source_quote: 99. No person shall, by way of business, provide administration services to a collective investment scheme unless the CIS manager or the collective investment scheme, as the case may be, seeks the prior approval of the Commission.
sections.relevance_reason: Regulator approval for financial data handling

sections.heading: Custodian licence
sections.title: Custodian licensing requirements for collective investment schemes
sections.summary: This section mandates that no person may hold assets of a collective investment scheme for safekeeping without holding a custodian licence. The Commission will only grant such a licence if the applicant is a bank or a bank subsidiary trust company and meets specified duties, obligations, and conditions outlined in FSC Rules. It also allows trustees under repealed trust acts to act as custodians if they comply with the Act and relevant regulations.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires custodian licensing for cross-border asset holding, impacting data transfer safeguards for collective investment schemes.

requirements.text: No person shall hold assets of a collective investment scheme for safekeeping without holding a custodian licence
Applicant must be a bank or a trust company that is a subsidiary of a bank
Applicant must meet requirements relating to duties and obligations, use of sub-custodians and other conditions as prescribed in FSC Rules
Trustees under repealed Unit Trust Act or Trusts Act may act as custodians if they comply with custodian requirements under this Act, regulations, and FSC Rules

sections.source_quote: 100. No person shall hold the assets of a collective investment scheme for safekeeping unless the person holds a custodian licence.
sections.relevance_reason: Custodian licensing for financial data handling

sections.heading: Duties of CIS managers and officers
sections.title: Duties of collective investment scheme managers and officers
sections.summary: CIS managers and officers must act honestly, exercise due care and diligence, prioritize participants' interests, treat participants equally, avoid misuse of information, ensure payments comply with scheme documents, and report breaches of the Act, regulations, or scheme constitutive documents to the Commission. Violations incur fines up to 500,000 rupees and imprisonment up to 5 years.
sections.dtia_summary: Imposes strict duties on CIS managers to prevent misuse of data and ensure transparency, critical for DTIA compliance in cross-border investment schemes.

requirements.text: CIS managers and officers must act honestly
CIS managers and officers must exercise the degree of care and diligence expected of a person in that position
CIS managers and officers must act in the best interests of participants and prioritize participants' interests over their own
CIS managers and officers must treat participants who hold interests of the same class equally and those of different classes fairly
CIS managers and officers must not use information acquired through their role to gain improper advantage for themselves or others or cause detriment to participants
CIS managers and officers must ensure all payments from scheme property comply with constitutive documents, this Act, regulations, and FSC Rules
CIS managers and officers must report breaches of this Act, regulations, or scheme constitutive documents to the Commission as soon as practicable

sections.source_quote: 105. CIS managers and officers shall act honestly; exercise care and diligence; act in best interests of participants; treat participants equally; not misuse information; ensure payments comply with scheme documents; report breaches to Commission.
sections.relevance_reason: CIS managers' duties include data handling and breach reporting

sections.heading: Disclosure by CIS and CIS managers
sections.title: Financial disclosure requirements for collective investment schemes
sections.summary: Collective investment schemes must file interim financial statements with the Commission and make them public within 45 days of the interim period's closing date, prepared in accordance with IFRS and other Financial Reporting Act standards. Annual reports must be filed within 90 days of the balance sheet date, including audited financial statements by approved audit firms. Non-compliance incurs fines up to 500,000 rupees.
sections.dtia_summary: Mandates timely financial disclosures for collective investment schemes, critical for DTIA compliance in cross-border data transfers involving financial reporting.

requirements.text: Collective investment schemes must file interim financial statements with Commission within 45 days of interim period closing
CIS managers must file interim financial statements with Commission within 45 days of interim period closing
Interim financial statements need not be audited
Collective investment schemes must file annual reports with Commission within 90 days of balance sheet date
Annual reports must include audited financial statements prepared in accordance with IFRS
Annual reports must be prepared by audit firms approved by Commission
Annual reports must be in form specified in FSC Rules
Collective investment schemes must file prospectus or disclosure document as specified in FSC Rules

sections.source_quote: 106. Every collective investment scheme shall file interim financial statements with Commission within 45 days of interim period closing, prepared in accordance with IFRS.
sections.relevance_reason: Financial data filing with penalties

sections.heading: Appointment and duties of auditors
sections.title: Auditor appointment and reporting duties
sections.summary: The Commission must approve audit firms for CIS managers and schemes only if they have adequate experience, expertise, and resources. Auditors must report to the Commission if they become aware of material adverse changes in risks, potential insolvency, contraventions of the Act, or criminal offences involving fraud or dishonesty. Auditors are protected from liability for communicating such information in good faith.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires auditors to monitor financial risks and report potential breaches, essential for DTIA compliance in cross-border financial data transfers.

requirements.text: Commission shall not approve audit firms unless they have adequate experience, expertise and resources
Commission may require auditors to submit additional information as necessary
Auditors must report to Commission if they become aware of material adverse changes in risks with potential to jeopardize going concern
Auditors must report to Commission if they become aware of contraventions of the Act, regulations, or FSC Rules
Auditors must report to Commission if they become aware of criminal offences involving fraud or dishonesty
Auditors must report to Commission if they become aware of serious irregularities
No duty of auditor is breached if they communicate information to Commission in good faith

sections.source_quote: 107. Commission shall not approve audit firms unless they have adequate experience, expertise and resources to carry out such an audit.
sections.relevance_reason: Auditor reporting duties on financial risks and fraud

sections.heading: Insider dealing prohibited
sections.title: Prohibition on insider dealing in securities
sections.summary: No person with inside information about securities of a reporting issuer may buy, sell, or trade in those securities, counsel others to do so, or disclose the information to others without authorization. Violations incur fines of 500,000 to 1,000,000 rupees and imprisonment up to 10 years. A defense exists if the person reasonably believed the information was publicly known.
sections.dtia_summary: Prohibits insider trading, critical for DTIA compliance in cross-border financial data transfers involving sensitive information.

requirements.text: No person with inside information may buy, sell, or otherwise deal in securities of a reporting issuer
No person with inside information may counsel, procure, or cause another person to deal in securities
No person with inside information may disclose information to others without authorization
A person informed of investment program by CIS manager or investment adviser is deemed to have acted in contravention if they use information for personal benefit
A defense exists if the person reasonably believed information was generally known to the public
Violations incur fines of 500,000 to 1,000,000 rupees and imprisonment up to 10 years

sections.source_quote: 111. No person who has inside information about securities of a reporting issuer shall buy, sell or otherwise deal in securities of that reporting issuer.
sections.relevance_reason: Tangential: financial misconduct, not direct data compliance

sections.heading: Market rigging
sections.title: Prohibition on market rigging activities
sections.summary: No person may engage in transactions that create or sustain artificial prices for securities on Mauritian exchanges or enter into fictitious transactions affecting security prices. Market rigging is a defense if the defendant proves they did not know and could not reasonably have known the transaction would affect prices. Underwriting activities by investment dealers during exchanges are exempt from this prohibition.
sections.dtia_summary: Prohibits market manipulation, critical for DTIA compliance in cross-border financial data transfers involving market stability.

requirements.text: No person may engage in transactions that create or sustain artificial prices for securities on Mauritian exchanges
No person may enter into fictitious or artificial transactions affecting security prices
Market rigging is a defense if defendant proves they did not know and could not reasonably have known the transaction would affect prices
Underwriting activities by investment dealers during exchanges are exempt from this prohibition

sections.source_quote: 114. No person shall engage in, or carry out, a transaction that creates or sustains an artificial price for securities on a securities exchange in Mauritius.
sections.relevance_reason: Purely financial regulation, no data handling aspects

sections.heading: Interpretation under this Part
sections.title: Definition of licensee and Chief Executive
sections.summary: This section defines key terms used in Part X of the Securities Act. A 'licensee' includes past and present licensees, their officers and partners, persons who ought to have been licensed, collective investment schemes, and SROs. The 'Chief Executive' includes any person designated by them or the Commission. The section also specifies that the Commission has additional powers from Part VIII and Sub-Parts A and B of Part IX of the Financial Services Act.

requirements.text: any reference to a 'licensee' shall include any person who has been a licensee
any reference to a 'licensee' shall include any person who is a present or past officer, partner or controller of the licensee
any reference to a 'licensee' shall include any person who ought to have been licensed under this Act
any reference to a 'licensee' shall include a collective investment scheme
any reference to a 'licensee' shall include an SRO
any reference to the 'Chief Executive' shall include any person designated by the Chief Executive or the Commission

sections.source_quote: 120. Interpretation under this Part (1) For the purposes of this Part— (a) any reference to a "licensee" shall include— (i) any person who has been a licensee; (ii) any person who is a present or past officer, partner or controller of the licensee; (iii) any person who ought to have been licensed under this Act; (iv) a collective investment scheme; (v) an SRO; (b) any reference to the "Chief Executive" shall include any person designated by the Chief Executive or the Commission.
sections.relevance_reason: Defines terms relevant to data compliance scope

sections.heading: Information relating to securities transactions
sections.title: Information sharing for securities transactions
sections.summary: The Chief Executive may direct persons involved in securities transactions to provide information to exchanges, public agencies, international bodies, foreign regulators, or law enforcement. This includes details about transaction participants, investment amounts, instructions, and associated persons. Failure to comply with such directions constitutes an offence.
sections.dtia_summary: This section enables cross-border data sharing for securities transactions, requiring information disclosure to foreign supervisory institutions and law enforcement agencies, which impacts DTIA requirements for international data transfers.

requirements.text: The Chief Executive may direct a person registered as the holder of securities to furnish information to any securities exchange, clearing and settlement facility, public sector agency, international organisation, foreign supervisory institution, or law enforcement agency
The information may include the name, address, contact details, and occupation of any party to the securities transaction
The information may include the nature, amount, and other details relating to the investment transaction
The information may include any instructions given to or by another party to the securities transaction
The information may include details of persons associated with or who received proceeds from the securities transaction
Any person who fails to comply with a direction under this section without reasonable cause commits an offence

sections.source_quote: 122. Information relating to securities transactions (1) In relation to any securities or any securities transaction, whether undertaken in or outside Mauritius, the Chief Executive may, without prejudice to his powers under the Financial Services Act, for the purposes of ensuring compliance with any— (a) relevant Act; (b) regulations made under a relevant Act; (c) FSC Rules; (d) rules approved by the Commission; or (e) agreement or arrangement for the exchange of information, direct— (i) a person registered as the holder of securities; (ii) a person whom the Commission has reasonable grounds to suspect that he— (A) is the holder of securities; (B) has a beneficial interest in securities; or (C) has acquired or disposed of securities, whether directly or through a nominee, trustee or agent; or (iii) any other person, to furnish information which the Chief Executive may, in furtherance of the objects and functions of the Commission, provide to any— (aa) securities exchange, clearing and settlement facility; (ab) public sector agency; (ac) international organisation; (ad) foreign supervisory institution; or (ae) law enforcement agency.
sections.relevance_reason: Regulator powers for financial data sharing

sections.heading: Cease trade order
sections.title: Cease trade orders for securities exchanges
sections.summary: The Commission may order a securities exchange to close for trading due to emergencies, natural disasters, or economic crises. The Commission must consult the exchange before issuing such orders and may direct the exchange during the closure. Violation of the order by investment dealers or licensed representatives results in fines up to one million rupees and imprisonment for up to five years.
sections.dtia_summary: This section allows the Commission to impose temporary trading halts across borders due to crises, impacting DTIA requirements for international market operations and cross-border data flows during emergencies.

requirements.text: The Commission may order that a securities exchange be closed for securities transactions
The Commission may make an order where orderly transaction of business on the securities exchange is adversely affected due to an emergency, natural disaster, or economic crisis
The Commission shall consult the securities exchange before making an order unless it is impracticable, undesirable, or inexpedient
The order shall remain in force for the period the Commission thinks fit, but not longer than the time the exchange is affected by the events specified
During the order period, the Commission may give directions to the securities exchange

Any investment dealer or licensed representative who deals in securities listed on an exchange during an order commits an offence and may be fined up to one million rupees and imprisoned for up to five years

sections.source_quote: 133. Cease trade order (1) Subject to subsections (2) and (3), the Commission may order that a securities exchange be closed for securities transactions. (2) The Commission may make an order under subsection (1) where it is of the opinion that the orderly transaction of business on the securities exchange is being or is likely to be adversely affected due to the occurrence of— (a) an emergency or a natural disaster; or (b) an economic or financial crisis.
sections.relevance_reason: Tangential: financial governance, not direct data compliance

sections.heading: Notification of decisions
sections.title: Notification of decisions
sections.summary: The Commission or Chief Executive must notify and publish decisions in a specified format, including the decision itself, reasons, findings on material facts, evidence, and the right to appeal to the Review Panel. SROs, exchanges, and facilities must also notify affected parties in writing when making decisions under their rules.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates transparent notification of decisions affecting data flows, ensuring stakeholders are informed of regulatory actions that could impact cross-border data transfers and international processing.

requirements.text: The Commission or Chief Executive shall give notice of and publish any decision in such form and manner as they think fit
An SRO, securities exchange, clearing and settlement facility, or trading facility shall give written notice of decisions to each person whose interests are affected
A notice shall set out the decision, reasons for the decision, findings on material questions of fact, evidence on which findings were based, and the right to appeal to the Review Panel if applicable

sections.source_quote: 135. Notification of decisions (1) Unless otherwise provided under this Act, the Financial Services Act, any regulations made under these Acts or any FSC Rules, the Commission or the Chief Executive, as the case may be, shall give notice of, and publish, any decision in such form and manner as it or he thinks fit. (2) Where an SRO, a securities exchange, a clearing and settlement facility or a trading facility makes a decision under its rules, it shall give written notice of the decision to each person whose interests are affected by the decision.
sections.relevance_reason: Regulatory notification requirements for financial data

sections.heading: Exchange of information
sections.title: Information exchange with foreign entities
sections.summary: The Commission may share information with securities exchanges, clearing facilities, or SROs for enforcement purposes, subject to conditions that restrict the use and disclosure of the information. This enables cross-border data sharing for regulatory compliance.
sections.dtia_summary: This section allows the Commission to exchange information with foreign entities for enforcement, requiring DTIA considerations for data sharing across borders with international regulatory bodies.

requirements.text: The Commission may give a securities exchange, clearing and settlement facility or any SRO information relevant to enforcement of this Act, regulations, or FSC Rules
Information given under this section may be subject to conditions restricting use and disclosure

sections.source_quote: 143. Exchange of information (1) Notwithstanding section 83 of the Financial Services Act, the Commission may give a securities exchange, a clearing and settlement facility or any SRO any information relevant to the enforcement of this Act, any regulations made under this Act or any FSC Rules for the purpose of discharging their respective functions.
sections.relevance_reason: Cross-border data sharing for financial compliance

sections.heading: Compensation fund
sections.title: Compensation fund for investors
sections.summary: A compensation fund must be established to cover pecuniary losses from licensees' insolvency, fraud, or bankruptcy. The fund is administered by the Commission through regulations, including contributions from licensees, claims settlement, and insurance policies. The fund may also be established by SROs with Commission approval.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes a compensation fund for investors, which may impact DTIA requirements for financial data handling and cross-border transfers of investor data in cases of insolvency or fraud.

requirements.text: There shall be established and maintained one or more compensation funds for purposes prescribed by regulations
A compensation fund may provide for compensation of investors who suffer pecuniary loss due to inability of a licensee to satisfy claims, fraud by a licensee, or insolvency of a licensee
A compensation fund shall be administered in accordance with regulations

Regulations may provide for management of the fund by a committee, levying contributions from licensees, subscribing to insurance policies, determining compensation quantum, settling claims, subrogation rights, and account-keeping
SROs, exchanges, or clearing facilities may establish compensation funds under their rules with Commission approval

sections.source_quote: 148. Compensation fund (1) There shall be established and maintained in such manner as may be prescribed, one or more compensation funds, for such purposes as may be prescribed.
sections.relevance_reason: Pure financial regulation, no data protection or cross-border transfer relevance

sections.heading: Publicity
sections.title: Public access to documents lodged with Commission
sections.summary: Any person may request to review or copy documents lodged with the Commission under this Act, regulations, or FSC Rules. The Commission may specify procedures, fees, and refuse access if the information is confidential with real commercial value or personal information in the public interest.
sections.dtia_summary: Allows public access to documents but restricts disclosure of confidential commercial information or personal data where public interest requires privacy.

requirements.text: Any person may review or copy documents lodged with the Commission under this Act, regulations, or FSC Rules
The Commission may specify reasonable procedures for requests and fees
The Commission may refuse access if information is confidential with real commercial value
The Commission may refuse access if personal information is in the public interest

sections.source_quote: 150. Publicity (1) Subject to this section, any person may, on request, review or copy any document lodged with the Commission under this Act, any regulations made under this Act or any FSC Rules.
sections.relevance_reason: Public access to personal data with refusal conditions

sections.heading: Immunity
sections.title: Immunity for good faith reports to Commission
sections.summary: Persons making good faith reports to the Commission under this Act, regulations, or FSC Rules are immune from legal action. They cannot face employment prejudice or penalties for reporting suspected breaches of this Act, regulations, or FSC Rules.
sections.dtia_summary: Provides immunity for reporting breaches, ensuring staff can report concerns without fear of retaliation.

requirements.text: No action shall lie against persons who make good faith reports to the Commission
No person shall face employment prejudice or penalties for reporting suspected breaches

sections.source_quote: 153. Immunity (1) No action shall lie against any person who makes a report in good faith to the Commission that is required or permitted under this Act...
sections.relevance_reason: Immunity for reporting breaches not data compliance

sections.heading: Regulations
sections.title: Regulations for Commission functions
sections.summary: The Minister may make regulations for this Act, amend the Schedule, and specify criteria for companies as SROs, takeover requirements, board duties, collective investment schemes, and penalties up to 2 million rupees.
sections.dtia_summary: Allows the Minister to create detailed rules for financial institutions, including penalties for breaches, affecting data handling and reporting.

requirements.text: The Minister may make regulations for the purposes of this Act
The Minister may amend the Schedule by regulations
Regulations may provide criteria for declaring companies as SROs
Regulations may specify takeover requirements
Regulations may define board duties and obligations
Regulations may cover collective investment schemes
Regulations may impose penalties up to 2 million rupees for breaches

sections.source_quote: 154. Regulations (1) The Minister may— (a) make such regulations, as he thinks fit for the purposes of this Act; (b) by regulations, amend the Schedule.
sections.relevance_reason: No data protection or cross-border transfer elements

sections.heading: FSC Rules
sections.title: FSC Rules for securities regulation
sections.summary: The Commission may publish FSC Rules covering prospectuses, financial statements, licensing, reporting requirements, prudential rules, and administrative penalties up to 2 million rupees for breaches.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines detailed rules for securities reporting and licensing, including penalties for non-compliance, affecting data handling and transparency.

requirements.text: The Commission may make FSC Rules published in the Gazette
FSC Rules may cover prospectuses and financial statements
FSC Rules may specify licensing criteria
FSC Rules may require reporting to the Commission
FSC Rules may impose prudential rules including capital requirements
FSC Rules may allow administrative penalties up to 2 million rupees

sections.source_quote: 155. FSC Rules (1) The Commission may, for the purposes of this Act, make rules to be known as FSC Rules and such rules shall be published in the Gazette.
sections.relevance_reason: No data protection or cross-border transfer elements

sections.heading: Transitional provisions – reporting issuers and insiders of reporting
sections.title: Transitional reporting requirements
sections.summary: Reporting issuers must file annual reports starting 6 months after Part VI commences and quarterly reports from the first quarter after 6 months. Insiders must file reports 3 months after the Act commences.
sections.dtia_summary: Sets transitional reporting timelines for financial institutions, affecting data retention and reporting obligations.

requirements.text: Reporting issuers must file annual reports as from the end of the financial year ending 6 months after Part VI commences
Reporting issuers must file quarterly reports as from the first quarter of the financial year starting 6 months after Part VI commences
Insiders must file reports 3 months after the Act commences

sections.source_quote: 161. Transitional provisions – reporting issuers and insiders of reporting issuers (1) Reporting issuers in existence immediately before the commencement of this Act, shall meet the requirements of— (a) filing of annual report as from the end of the financial year ending 6 months after the commencement of Part VI;
sections.relevance_reason: Pure reporting requirements, no data compliance elements

sections.heading: A document issued or executed by a bank or deposit taking non-bank
sections.title: Bank-issued debt acknowledgment documents
sections.summary: A document issued by a bank or deposit-taking non-bank financial institution licensed under the Banking Act that acknowledges indebtedness in the ordinary course of banking or deposit-taking business.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines specific debt acknowledgment documents issued by financial institutions, relevant for data classification and handling.

requirements.text: A document issued or executed by a bank or deposit taking non-bank financial institution licensed under the Banking Act

sections.source_quote: 15. A document issued or executed by a bank or deposit taking non-bank financial institution licensed or authorised under the Banking Act, being a document issued or executed in the ordinary course of its banking or deposit taking business, that acknowledges the indebtedness of the bank or institution arising in the ordinary course of that business.
sections.relevance_reason: Banking license requirement for financial data

sections.heading: A foreign exchange contract as defined in the Banking Act.
sections.title: Foreign exchange contracts defined under Banking Act
sections.summary: This section defines a foreign exchange contract as per the Banking Act. The definition is used to determine what constitutes a foreign exchange contract for regulatory purposes. The definition is specific to the context of the Securities Act.

requirements.text: A foreign exchange contract as defined in the Banking Act
